answers:
**1. Setting-by-setting review of `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` (lines 1-8) — every value in this file is a verbatim copy of `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars` and none of them belong in production:**

- `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR = "true"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:2`) — a developer-convenience flag. Per the do-not-inherit scrub, this should be **omitted from prod entirely**, not set to `"false"`. `backend/config.py:8` reads it directly as a boolean gate for debug tooling; there's no reason a production app service should even have this key defined.
- `AUTH_RELAXED = "true"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:3`) — relaxed auth is a staging-only convenience. `backend/config.py:9` turns this into `auth_relaxed: True` in production right now. This must be removed (key omitted), not flipped to `"false"`.
- `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS = "qa@example.com:superadmin"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:4`) — an allow-listed test account with superadmin privileges, wired into `backend/config.py:10` (`preset_admin_accounts`). This is exactly the "allow-list of internal/test accounts with elevated privileges" the scrub calls out; it must be removed, giving a production QA login superadmin access otherwise.
- `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED = "false"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:5`) — looks safe (disabled), but it is **not** actually safe: `backend/config.py:19-22`, `record_shadow_observation()` checks only `cfg["shadow_scoring_sink"]` — it never consults `shadow_scoring_enabled` at all. So this flag is dead/misleading in the code path that matters.
- `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK = "s3://medislot-shadow/staging/observations/"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:6`) — because of the bug above, this non-empty value is what actually re-arms shadow scoring in prod despite the "disabled" flag, and it would write production clinic data into a bucket path literally named `staging/observations/`. This key must be fully absent in prod, matching the skill's warning that "a stray non-empty value elsewhere in the same config can silently re-arm" a shadow/experimental feature.
- `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN = "arn:synthetic:secrets:legacy-billing-key"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:7`) — referenced only in `backend/config.py:13` as a passthrough value with no other consumer visible in this repo; this matches the skill's "secret injection that's a known dead fossil from a previous architecture" pattern and should not be recreated in prod without confirming it's actually needed by production billing code.
- `infra/prod/main.tf:3`, `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"` — this is the most serious one. Prod's deploy-role trust policy is **identical** to staging's, not scoped to `environment:production`. Per the skill ("a stricter environment needs its own scoped trust condition, never an inherited... condition shared with a looser environment"), this must be changed to `"repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production"`. As written, prod's IAM role trusts the *staging* GitHub Actions OIDC identity, and — combined with the deploy-role resource in `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7` being named `"medislot-deploy"` with no environment suffix in either caller — staging and prod are set up to create/manage an identically-named role, which is a collision risk if both are applied against the same account.

**2. Frontend build/deploy path and where the API base URL comes from:**

The frontend is a Vite/React app (`frontend/vite.config.ts:3` comment: "VITE_-prefixed variables are inlined into the built bundle at build time"; `frontend/src.txt:1-2` confirms the app reads `import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL` at build time). The pipeline (`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`) has a single `build-frontend` job that runs `npm ci` then `npx vite build --mode staging` (line 14) exactly once. Vite's `--mode staging` picks up `frontend/.env.staging`, whose only content is `VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://staging-api.example.com` (`frontend/.env.staging:1`) — so the staging API URL gets baked directly into the compiled JS bundle at that point; it is not read at runtime. That same build is then packaged into a container image and pushed once (`docker build`/`docker push` to `registry.example.com/medislot:latest`, lines 18-21), producing one digest. Both `deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` jobs (lines 22-35) then deploy **that same digest** — `deploy-prod`'s step is literally commented "deploy the same already-built digest to prod" (line 34). There is no `frontend/.env.production` anywhere in the repo (confirmed by `find`), and no per-environment rebuild step. This is exactly the "rebuild per environment; don't promote a build artifact wholesale" trap: as this pipeline is written today, the production container will ship the frontend bundle hardcoded to call `https://staging-api.example.com`, with no runtime override available once compiled.

**3. Deploy pipeline walkthrough for the first production release, and what's missing:**

Per `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`, on every push to `main`: (a) `build-frontend` checks out, `npm ci`+`vite build --mode staging`, builds one Docker image and pushes it to `registry.example.com/medislot:latest`, capturing its digest; (b) `deploy-staging` (gated by GitHub `environment: staging`) runs `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> staging`; (c) `deploy-prod` (gated by GitHub `environment: production`) then runs `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> prod`, depending on both prior jobs.

Two things this release requires are missing:
- `scripts/deploy.sh` does not exist anywhere in this repository — `git ls-files` shows no such path, and `git log --all --full-history -- '**/deploy.sh'` returns nothing, so it was never committed on any branch. Both `deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` invoke a script that isn't there; every deploy job as currently written would fail at that step. (Staging's live `terraform.tfstate` shows staging infra *was* applied at some point, but that doesn't prove this exact pipeline version, with this missing script, has ever successfully run end-to-end — it's equally possible staging was stood up manually or by an earlier script version that's since been deleted.)
- There is also no `Dockerfile` anywhere in the repo (`find . -iname "Dockerfile*"` returned nothing), yet the build step runs `docker build -t ... .` — that step has no build context to succeed with, either.
- No step in the pipeline runs database migrations. `docs/prod-runbook.md:6` claims "Database migrations are applied automatically during deploy," but that's not backed up by anything in `deploy.yml`, and it can't be verified against the missing `deploy.sh` either. Separately, all three existing migrations (`backend/migrations/versions/0001_base.py`, `0002_tenants.py`, `0003_billing.py`) have `upgrade()` bodies that are just `pass` — no-ops — so even if migrations do run automatically, they currently create no schema. Given `infra/prod/` has never been applied (no `terraform.tfstate` there), this launch needs both a first Terraform apply and a confirmed, explicit first migration pass — neither is provided by this pipeline as it stands.
- `docs/prod-runbook.md` itself is stale ("last updated: 3 months ago"), predating even the prod-infra commit (`2f6572d`), so its claims about automation should be re-verified rather than trusted.

**4. CI authentication for staging vs. production:**

The mechanism is GitHub Actions OIDC federation into a cloud IAM role, evidenced by the `oidc_sub_condition` on `synthetic_iam_role.deploy` in `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:6-11`, which checks a `token.actions.example.com:sub` claim against a `repo:<org>/<repo>:environment:<name>` string — the standard shape of a GitHub Actions OIDC token `sub` claim when a job uses `environment:` protection. In `deploy.yml`, the `deploy-staging` job sets `environment: staging` (line 25) and `deploy-prod` sets `environment: production` (line 32), so GitHub will mint OIDC tokens with `sub = repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging` and `...environment:production` respectively.

For staging, this lines up: `infra/staging/main.tf:3` sets `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`, matching the token staging's job will present, and staging's `terraform.tfstate` shows this role has actually been created and is presumably working today.

For production, this will **not** work correctly as configured: `infra/prod/main.tf:3` sets the *same* `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"` (not `environment:production`) — i.e., prod's deploy role, per its own Terraform, will only trust tokens whose `sub` claims `environment:staging`, not the `environment:production` token GitHub will actually issue to the `deploy-prod` job. If applied as-is, the prod deploy job's OIDC token would be rejected by its own role's trust policy (or, if the identically-named `medislot-deploy` role from staging's apply is what's actually live in the account, the two environments would be sharing one trust policy scoped to staging, which is the exact anti-pattern the do-not-inherit scrub warns against: "a stricter environment needs its own scoped trust condition, never an inherited... condition shared with a looser environment"). Separately, `deploy.yml` declares no `permissions: id-token: write` block at all, which is normally required for a job to request an OIDC token in the first place — I can't confirm whether an org/repo default permission covers this, since that setting isn't visible from repo contents alone.
report:
I now have everything needed for a grounded report.

## Plan

Review MediSlot's production cutover before any `terraform apply`, following `.claude/skills/staging-to-prod-cutover-campaign/SKILL.md`: (1) probe live state rather than trust docs, (2) run the "do-not-inherit" scrub on prod config copied from staging, (3) check whether the frontend bundle is rebuilt per-environment or promoted wholesale, (4) verify ops scripts the pipeline calls actually exist, (5) check CI auth/trust-policy scoping between environments. Scope: the `medislot/` repo (the only MediSlot code tree; other top-level directories are unrelated sibling projects). Read-only throughout — no applies, no writes.

## Actions

- Read `.claude/skills/staging-to-prod-cutover-campaign/SKILL.md` in full — supplied the checklist above (do-not-inherit scrub, rebuild-per-environment, ops-script existence check, deploy-role trust scoping, migration-automation assumption).
- `find` on the working directory — confirmed `medislot/` is the target repo among several unrelated sibling repos.
- `git status`, `git branch -a`, `git log --oneline --all -20` in `medislot/` — single branch `main`, working tree clean, two commits: `28f07ee` (staging environment/pipeline/runbook) and `2f6572d` ("REL-140 add production infra (copied from staging)"). No unmerged prod branch to worry about.
- Read `infra/staging/main.tf` and `infra/prod/main.tf` — both call `module.deploy_role` with identical `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`, and the module's `synthetic_iam_role` resource name (`medislot-deploy`) is hardcoded, not parameterized by environment.
- Read `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars` and `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` — byte-for-byte identical `app_settings` maps.
- Read `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf` — confirms `oidc_sub_condition` is the sole trust-policy input, no environment-specific defaulting.
- Read `infra/prod/README.md` — states prod Terraform was "copied from infra/staging ahead of first launch (REL-140). Not yet applied."
- `ls -la infra/prod/` and `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate` — staging has a `terraform.tfstate` file (has been applied); prod directory has no state file at all (confirms it has never been applied, corroborating the README).
- Read `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate` — shows staging's live `synthetic_iam_role` name is `medislot-deploy` and `synthetic_app_service` name is `medislot-staging`.
- Read `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` — the only workflow file in the repo (`ls .github/workflows/`).
- Read `frontend/vite.config.ts`, `frontend/.env.staging`, `frontend/src.txt` — establishes how `VITE_API_BASE_URL` gets into the built bundle.
- `find` for `.env.production` / any prod env file under `frontend/` — none exists.
- Read `backend/config.py` — shows how the env vars from `app.auto.tfvars` are consumed at runtime, including the shadow-scoring sink logic.
- Read `docs/prod-runbook.md` — states deploys are automated and "database migrations are applied automatically during deploy," dated "last updated: 3 months ago" (predates the prod infra even being added).
- `git ls-files` and `git log --all --full-history -- '**/deploy.sh'` in `medislot/` — `scripts/deploy.sh`, which `deploy.yml` invokes, has never existed in this repo at any commit. Also confirmed no `Dockerfile` exists anywhere in the repo despite the workflow running `docker build -t ... .`.
- Read the three files under `backend/migrations/versions/` — all three `upgrade()` bodies are `pass` (no-op stubs), and `grep` for a migration-running step in `deploy.yml` found none.
- `grep` for `permissions:` / `id-token` in `deploy.yml` — none declared in the workflow file itself.
- `grep` for a Terraform `backend "..."` block anywhere under `infra/` — none found, meaning both staging and prod use local state by default (explains the local `terraform.tfstate` file in `infra/staging/`).

## Answers

**1. Setting-by-setting review of `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` (lines 1-8) — every value in this file is a verbatim copy of `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars` and none of them belong in production:**

- `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR = "true"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:2`) — a developer-convenience flag. Per the do-not-inherit scrub, this should be **omitted from prod entirely**, not set to `"false"`. `backend/config.py:8` reads it directly as a boolean gate for debug tooling; there's no reason a production app service should even have this key defined.

- `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN = "arn:synthetic:secrets:legacy-billing-key"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:7`) — referenced only in `backend/config.py:13` as a passthrough value with no other consumer visible in this repo; this matches the skill's "secret injection that's a known dead fossil from a previous architecture" pattern and should not be recreated in prod without confirming it's actually needed by production billing code.

**2. Frontend build/deploy path and where the API base URL comes from:**

**3. Deploy pipeline walkthrough for the first production release, and what's missing:**

Two things this release requires are missing:

(Staging's live `terraform.tfstate` shows staging infra *was* applied at some point, but that doesn't prove this exact pipeline version, with this missing script, has ever successfully run end-to-end — it's equally possible staging was stood up manually or by an earlier script version that's since been deleted.)

- No step in the pipeline runs database migrations. `docs/prod-runbook.md:6` claims "Database migrations are applied automatically during deploy," but that's not backed up by anything in `deploy.yml`, and it can't be verified against the missing `deploy.sh` either. For staging, this lines up: `infra/staging/main.tf:3` sets `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`, matching the token staging's job will present, and staging's `terraform.tfstate` shows this role has actually been created and is presumably working today.

For production, this will **not** work correctly as configured: `infra/prod/main.tf:3` sets the *same* `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"` (not `environment:production`) — i.e., prod's deploy role, per its own Terraform, will only trust tokens whose `sub` claims `environment:staging`, not the `environment:production` token GitHub will actually issue to the `deploy-prod` job. ## Next steps

If I were allowed to make changes, in this order:
1. Fix `infra/prod/main.tf:3` to `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production"`, and parameterize the `synthetic_iam_role` name in `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7` per environment (e.g. `"medislot-deploy-${var.environment}"`) so staging and prod never collide on the same role name.
2. Rewrite `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` to omit `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR`, `AUTH_RELAXED`, `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS`, `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED`, and `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK` entirely, and confirm with whoever owns billing whether `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN` is genuinely needed in prod before recreating it.
3. Fix `backend/config.py`'s `record_shadow_observation` to actually gate on `shadow_scoring_enabled`, as defense-in-depth even after the sink var is removed from prod.
4. Add `frontend/.env.production` with the real production API URL, and change the pipeline to build twice — once per environment — instead of promoting one digest to both; add an automated grep/scan of the built bundle for `staging-api.example.com` before it ships, per the skill's guidance.
5. Locate or author `scripts/deploy.sh` and a `Dockerfile`, verify they're actually committed and included in whatever the build step needs, and confirm with the team whether `deploy.sh` runs migrations — if not, add an explicit migration step and flesh out the currently-empty `upgrade()` bodies before first launch.
6. Update `docs/prod-runbook.md` to reflect verified (not assumed) behavior, and re-date it.
7. Before any `terraform apply` in `infra/prod/`, get an explicit go-ahead from the infra/account owner per the skill's hard rule — this analysis alone doesn't authorize applying.

## Confidence and gaps

High confidence, directly observed in files: the `app.auto.tfvars` values being byte-identical between staging/prod, the `oidc_sub_condition` staging-string bug in `infra/prod/main.tf`, the shadow-scoring enable/sink logic bug in `backend/config.py`, the single-build/dual-deploy structure and missing `.env.production` in `deploy.yml`/`frontend/`, the total absence of `scripts/deploy.sh` and any `Dockerfile` in the repo's full git history, and the no-op migration bodies.

Gaps I could not verify from repo contents alone: (a) whether GitHub org/repo settings grant `id-token: write` by default (the workflow file itself doesn't declare it); (b) whether staging's deploy has ever actually succeeded end-to-end through this exact pipeline version, given `deploy.sh` is missing — the `terraform.tfstate` proves *something* applied staging's Terraform at some point, but not via this CI file; (c) whether `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN` is truly dead or actually load-bearing elsewhere (no consumer beyond the passthrough in `config.py` is visible in this repo); (d) the actual cloud account topology (whether staging and prod Terraform target the same or different accounts) is not determinable from these files, which affects how serious the role-name-collision risk in `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf` really is. All of these should be confirmed live per the skill's "probe, don't assume" step before any apply.

- `git ls-files` and `git log --all --full-history -- '**/deploy.sh'` in `medislot/` — `scripts/deploy.sh`, which `deploy.yml` invokes, has never existed in this repo at any commit. Also confirmed no `Dockerfile` exists anywhere in the repo despite the workflow running `docker build -t ... .`.
- Read the three files under `backend/migrations/versions/` — all three `upgrade()` bodies are `pass` (no-op stubs), and `grep` for a migration-running step in `deploy.yml` found none.
- `grep` for `permissions:` / `id-token` in `deploy.yml` — none declared in the workflow file itself.
- `grep` for a Terraform `backend "..."` block anywhere under `infra/` — none found, meaning both staging and prod use local state by default (explains the local `terraform.tfstate` file in `infra/staging/`).
sections.answers:
**1. Setting-by-setting review of `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` (lines 1-8) — every value in this file is a verbatim copy of `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars` and none of them belong in production:**

- `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR = "true"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:2`) — a developer-convenience flag. Per the do-not-inherit scrub, this should be **omitted from prod entirely**, not set to `"false"`. `backend/config.py:8` reads it directly as a boolean gate for debug tooling; there's no reason a production app service should even have this key defined.
- `AUTH_RELAXED = "true"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:3`) — relaxed auth is a staging-only convenience. `backend/config.py:9` turns this into `auth_relaxed: True` in production right now. This must be removed (key omitted), not flipped to `"false"`.
- `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS = "qa@example.com:superadmin"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:4`) — an allow-listed test account with superadmin privileges, wired into `backend/config.py:10` (`preset_admin_accounts`). This is exactly the "allow-list of internal/test accounts with elevated privileges" the scrub calls out; it must be removed, giving a production QA login superadmin access otherwise.
- `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED = "false"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:5`) — looks safe (disabled), but it is **not** actually safe: `backend/config.py:19-22`, `record_shadow_observation()` checks only `cfg["shadow_scoring_sink"]` — it never consults `shadow_scoring_enabled` at all. So this flag is dead/misleading in the code path that matters.
- `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK = "s3://medislot-shadow/staging/observations/"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:6`) — because of the bug above, this non-empty value is what actually re-arms shadow scoring in prod despite the "disabled" flag, and it would write production clinic data into a bucket path literally named `staging/observations/`. This key must be fully absent in prod, matching the skill's warning that "a stray non-empty value elsewhere in the same config can silently re-arm" a shadow/experimental feature.
- `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN = "arn:synthetic:secrets:legacy-billing-key"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:7`) — referenced only in `backend/config.py:13` as a passthrough value with no other consumer visible in this repo; this matches the skill's "secret injection that's a known dead fossil from a previous architecture" pattern and should not be recreated in prod without confirming it's actually needed by production billing code.
- `infra/prod/main.tf:3`, `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"` — this is the most serious one. Prod's deploy-role trust policy is **identical** to staging's, not scoped to `environment:production`. Per the skill ("a stricter environment needs its own scoped trust condition, never an inherited... condition shared with a looser environment"), this must be changed to `"repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production"`. As written, prod's IAM role trusts the *staging* GitHub Actions OIDC identity, and — combined with the deploy-role resource in `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7` being named `"medislot-deploy"` with no environment suffix in either caller — staging and prod are set up to create/manage an identically-named role, which is a collision risk if both are applied against the same account.

**2. Frontend build/deploy path and where the API base URL comes from:**

The frontend is a Vite/React app (`frontend/vite.config.ts:3` comment: "VITE_-prefixed variables are inlined into the built bundle at build time"; `frontend/src.txt:1-2` confirms the app reads `import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL` at build time). The pipeline (`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`) has a single `build-frontend` job that runs `npm ci` then `npx vite build --mode staging` (line 14) exactly once. Vite's `--mode staging` picks up `frontend/.env.staging`, whose only content is `VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://staging-api.example.com` (`frontend/.env.staging:1`) — so the staging API URL gets baked directly into the compiled JS bundle at that point; it is not read at runtime. That same build is then packaged into a container image and pushed once (`docker build`/`docker push` to `registry.example.com/medislot:latest`, lines 18-21), producing one digest. Both `deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` jobs (lines 22-35) then deploy **that same digest** — `deploy-prod`'s step is literally commented "deploy the same already-built digest to prod" (line 34). There is no `frontend/.env.production` anywhere in the repo (confirmed by `find`), and no per-environment rebuild step. This is exactly the "rebuild per environment; don't promote a build artifact wholesale" trap: as this pipeline is written today, the production container will ship the frontend bundle hardcoded to call `https://staging-api.example.com`, with no runtime override available once compiled.

**3. Deploy pipeline walkthrough for the first production release, and what's missing:**

Per `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`, on every push to `main`: (a) `build-frontend` checks out, `npm ci`+`vite build --mode staging`, builds one Docker image and pushes it to `registry.example.com/medislot:latest`, capturing its digest; (b) `deploy-staging` (gated by GitHub `environment: staging`) runs `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> staging`; (c) `deploy-prod` (gated by GitHub `environment: production`) then runs `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> prod`, depending on both prior jobs.

Two things this release requires are missing:
- `scripts/deploy.sh` does not exist anywhere in this repository — `git ls-files` shows no such path, and `git log --all --full-history -- '**/deploy.sh'` returns nothing, so it was never committed on any branch. Both `deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` invoke a script that isn't there; every deploy job as currently written would fail at that step. (Staging's live `terraform.tfstate` shows staging infra *was* applied at some point, but that doesn't prove this exact pipeline version, with this missing script, has ever successfully run end-to-end — it's equally possible staging was stood up manually or by an earlier script version that's since been deleted.)
- There is also no `Dockerfile` anywhere in the repo (`find . -iname "Dockerfile*"` returned nothing), yet the build step runs `docker build -t ... .` — that step has no build context to succeed with, either.
- No step in the pipeline runs database migrations. `docs/prod-runbook.md:6` claims "Database migrations are applied automatically during deploy," but that's not backed up by anything in `deploy.yml`, and it can't be verified against the missing `deploy.sh` either. Separately, all three existing migrations (`backend/migrations/versions/0001_base.py`, `0002_tenants.py`, `0003_billing.py`) have `upgrade()` bodies that are just `pass` — no-ops — so even if migrations do run automatically, they currently create no schema. Given `infra/prod/` has never been applied (no `terraform.tfstate` there), this launch needs both a first Terraform apply and a confirmed, explicit first migration pass — neither is provided by this pipeline as it stands.
- `docs/prod-runbook.md` itself is stale ("last updated: 3 months ago"), predating even the prod-infra commit (`2f6572d`), so its claims about automation should be re-verified rather than trusted.

**4. CI authentication for staging vs. production:**

The mechanism is GitHub Actions OIDC federation into a cloud IAM role, evidenced by the `oidc_sub_condition` on `synthetic_iam_role.deploy` in `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:6-11`, which checks a `token.actions.example.com:sub` claim against a `repo:<org>/<repo>:environment:<name>` string — the standard shape of a GitHub Actions OIDC token `sub` claim when a job uses `environment:` protection. In `deploy.yml`, the `deploy-staging` job sets `environment: staging` (line 25) and `deploy-prod` sets `environment: production` (line 32), so GitHub will mint OIDC tokens with `sub = repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging` and `...environment:production` respectively.

For staging, this lines up: `infra/staging/main.tf:3` sets `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`, matching the token staging's job will present, and staging's `terraform.tfstate` shows this role has actually been created and is presumably working today.

For production, this will **not** work correctly as configured: `infra/prod/main.tf:3` sets the *same* `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"` (not `environment:production`) — i.e., prod's deploy role, per its own Terraform, will only trust tokens whose `sub` claims `environment:staging`, not the `environment:production` token GitHub will actually issue to the `deploy-prod` job. If applied as-is, the prod deploy job's OIDC token would be rejected by its own role's trust policy (or, if the identically-named `medislot-deploy` role from staging's apply is what's actually live in the account, the two environments would be sharing one trust policy scoped to staging, which is the exact anti-pattern the do-not-inherit scrub warns against: "a stricter environment needs its own scoped trust condition, never an inherited... condition shared with a looser environment"). Separately, `deploy.yml` declares no `permissions: id-token: write` block at all, which is normally required for a job to request an OIDC token in the first place — I can't confirm whether an org/repo default permission covers this, since that setting isn't visible from repo contents alone.
sections.confidence and gaps:
High confidence, directly observed in files: the `app.auto.tfvars` values being byte-identical between staging/prod, the `oidc_sub_condition` staging-string bug in `infra/prod/main.tf`, the shadow-scoring enable/sink logic bug in `backend/config.py`, the single-build/dual-deploy structure and missing `.env.production` in `deploy.yml`/`frontend/`, the total absence of `scripts/deploy.sh` and any `Dockerfile` in the repo's full git history, and the no-op migration bodies.

Gaps I could not verify from repo contents alone: (a) whether GitHub org/repo settings grant `id-token: write` by default (the workflow file itself doesn't declare it); (b) whether staging's deploy has ever actually succeeded end-to-end through this exact pipeline version, given `deploy.sh` is missing — the `terraform.tfstate` proves *something* applied staging's Terraform at some point, but not via this CI file; (c) whether `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN` is truly dead or actually load-bearing elsewhere (no consumer beyond the passthrough in `config.py` is visible in this repo); (d) the actual cloud account topology (whether staging and prod Terraform target the same or different accounts) is not determinable from these files, which affects how serious the role-name-collision risk in `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf` really is. All of these should be confirmed live per the skill's "probe, don't assume" step before any apply.
sections.next steps:
If I were allowed to make changes, in this order:
1. Fix `infra/prod/main.tf:3` to `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production"`, and parameterize the `synthetic_iam_role` name in `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7` per environment (e.g. `"medislot-deploy-${var.environment}"`) so staging and prod never collide on the same role name.
2. Rewrite `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` to omit `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR`, `AUTH_RELAXED`, `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS`, `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED`, and `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK` entirely, and confirm with whoever owns billing whether `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN` is genuinely needed in prod before recreating it.
3. Fix `backend/config.py`'s `record_shadow_observation` to actually gate on `shadow_scoring_enabled`, as defense-in-depth even after the sink var is removed from prod.
4. Add `frontend/.env.production` with the real production API URL, and change the pipeline to build twice — once per environment — instead of promoting one digest to both; add an automated grep/scan of the built bundle for `staging-api.example.com` before it ships, per the skill's guidance.
5. Locate or author `scripts/deploy.sh` and a `Dockerfile`, verify they're actually committed and included in whatever the build step needs, and confirm with the team whether `deploy.sh` runs migrations — if not, add an explicit migration step and flesh out the currently-empty `upgrade()` bodies before first launch.
6. Update `docs/prod-runbook.md` to reflect verified (not assumed) behavior, and re-date it.
7. Before any `terraform apply` in `infra/prod/`, get an explicit go-ahead from the infra/account owner per the skill's hard rule — this analysis alone doesn't authorize applying.
sections.plan: Review MediSlot's production cutover before any `terraform apply`, following `.claude/skills/staging-to-prod-cutover-campaign/SKILL.md`: (1) probe live state rather than trust docs, (2) run the "do-not-inherit" scrub on prod config copied from staging, (3) check whether the frontend bundle is rebuilt per-environment or promoted wholesale, (4) verify ops scripts the pipeline calls actually exist, (5) check CI auth/trust-policy scoping between environments. Scope: the `medislot/` repo (the only MediSlot code tree; other top-level directories are unrelated sibling projects). Read-only throughout — no applies, no writes.
